entry.title: Building Custom Joule Capabilities: Role-Based Scenario Access in Joule
entry.content:
Introduction

This post is part of a series on extending SAP Joule with custom capabilities by using Joule Studio CLI. If you are new to Joule capability development or need help setting up your environment, start with Building Custom Joule Capabilities: Getting Started.

Assume you've built your first Joule capability which includes scenarios such as one functionality to return salary information. The scenarios are well-described, the functions return useful data, and everything works in your test environment. Then someone from HR asks: "Wait, can every employee see the salary report scenario when they talk to Joule?"

That's the moment the most people realize they need access control.

The problem is straightforward: in a customized Joule capability developed using Joule Studio CLI  every scenario is visible to every end user. That's fine for general-purpose skills, but the moment you're surfacing sensitive data (compensation figures, financial forecasts, headcount plans, performance ratings) you need to ensure that Joule only presents those scenarios to the people authorized to use them.

The solution is visibility_condition, a declarative field you add directly to a scenario YAML. When a user sends a message to Joule, the platform evaluates each scenario's visibility condition against the user's identity token before even considering whether the scenario matches the intent. If the condition evaluates to false, the scenario is completely invisible to that user. It won't match, it won't respond, and it won't appear in any suggestions.

In this post, you'll build a small HR capability with two scenarios: one that any employee can access (general HR information like office locations and holiday schedules), and one restricted to members of an IAS group called HR_Managers (salary reports and compensation data). By the end, you'll understand exactly how to gate any scenario behind group membership, and you'll know the full range of attributes you can use to build more sophisticated access patterns.

Note: The complete source for this capability, along with all other examples from this series, is available on GitHub.

 

How Visibility Conditions Work

Before writing any YAML, it's worth understanding what Joule actually does with a visibility_condition at runtime.

When a user sends a message, Joule's orchestration layer retrieves the user's IAS identity token. It then iterates over all scenarios registered in the active capability deployment and evaluates each scenario's visibility_condition against the token. Scenarios that pass the condition (or have no condition at all) are added to the candidate pool for intent matching. Scenarios that fail are dropped entirely. They don't participate in matching, and the user has no indication they exist.

This means the access control is enforced before any LLM-based intent matching happens. You're not relying on the model to "know" not to use a restricted scenario. The scenario is structurally unavailable.

The default behavior (no visibility_condition field) is equivalent to always visible. Any scenario without this field is included in the candidate pool for every user.

Joule currently supports two condition object types:

  • ibn_targets: Gates a scenario on SAP Work Zone navigation targets. Useful for capabilities that should only appear when the user has access to a specific Work Zone application or tile.
  • ias_attributes: Gates a scenario on attributes present in the user's IAS identity token. This is the focus of this post.

For most access control use cases, ias_attributes is what you want. It reads directly from the identity token that IAS issues when the user authenticates, which means it reflects your authoritative identity system with no additional configuration required on the Joule side.

 

Prerequisites

This post assumes you have the Joule CLI installed and the other prerequisites mentioned in the overview blog post. If you haven't done that yet, start with the first blog post in this series, which covers CLI installation, BTP subaccount configuration, and your first deployment end-to-end.

For this walkthrough specifically, you'll need:

  • IAS admin access in the tenant connected to your Joule deployment. You need permission to create groups and assign users.
  • Two test user accounts: one that you'll add to the HR_Managers group (your "HR Manager" persona), and one that you'll leave unassigned (your "regular employee" persona). These users should've access to the end_user BTP Role to be able to access Joule itself.
  • The Joule CLI authenticated and pointed at your development subaccount.

 

Step 1: Configure IAS Groups

IAS group membership is the attribute we'll use to gate the salary scenario. Before writing any YAML, set up the group and assign your test user.

Log in to your IAS admin console at https://<your-tenant>.accounts.ondemand.com/admin. Navigate to Applications & Resources in the left-hand menu, then select Groups.

Click Create and fill in the group name exactly as HR_MANAGERS. The name is case-sensitive. The value in your scenario YAML must match it precisely. Add a description and save.

Next, create your HR Manager test user. Navigate to Users & Authorizations > User Management and click Add User. Fill in the details and set the email to whatever address you want to use for testing.

Important for fictive email addresses: When creating a test user with a made-up email address, make sure to select Set initial password rather than Send activation email. An activation email sent to a non-existent address will never arrive, and the account will be stuck in an unactivated state.

After the user logs in for the first time, IAS will prompt them to set a new password. Once they do, IAS sends a verification email to the address on file. For a fictive address this will never arrive, leaving the account in an unverified state that can cause login issues. To work around this, go back to the user's profile in the IAS admin console and check Verify Email directly. This marks the email address as verified without sending any email.

With the user created, open their profile and go to the Groups tab. Click Add and assign them to HR_MANAGERS. Save the changes.

If you'd rather not create a second user at all, you can assign the HR_MANAGERS group to your own IAS user account instead. That lets you test the restricted scenario directly without managing a separate persona. To test the regular employee path, simply remove yourself from the group and log back in.

Leave your regular employee test user with no group assignment. You don't need to do anything to "deny" access. The absence of the group membership is sufficient. The visibility condition will evaluate to false for any user not in the listed group.

One important note: IAS group membership is reflected in the identity token that gets issued at login time. If you assign a user to a group while they have an active session, they may need to log out and log back in before the change takes effect in Joule. Keep this in mind when you're testing.

 

Step 2: The Capability File

The capability file is minimal. We have two scenarios and two functions, and since both functions use simple message actions (no external system calls), we don't need any system_aliases. This keeps the focus squarely on the access control pattern.

schema_version: 3.27.0
metadata:
  namespace: joule.ext
  name: hr_reports_capability
  version: 1.0.0
  display_name: HR Reports
  description: Demonstrates role-based scenario access using IAS group attributes

The namespace: joule.ext marks this as an extension capability to be able to be deployed to the unified central Joule instance (sap_digital_assistant).

Note that the schema version is 3.27.0. If you're on an older version of the CLI and it complains, you can lower this. Just make sure you're not below 3.23.0 if you want to use sap_licenses as an attribute, or below 3.15.0 if you want to combine ias_attributes with ibn_targets conditions.

 

Step 3: The Public Scenario

The public scenario has no visibility_condition, which means it's included in the candidate pool for every user regardless of their identity attributes.

description: >
  Get general HR information including company policies, office locations,
  and the annual holiday schedule for all employees.
target:
  name: public_info
  type: function

The description field is the primary input to Joule's intent matching. Write it as a natural-language description of what a user would ask to trigger this scenario. Joule compares the user's message against this description semantically, so be specific about the kinds of questions it handles. Something like "company policies, office locations, and the annual holiday schedule" gives the model clear signal about what belongs here.

The target points to the public_info function by name. 

Any end user can ask Joule about holiday schedules, office locations, or company policies, and this scenario will be available to match their intent.

 

Step 4: The Restricted Scenario: Deep Dive

The restricted scenario is where the interesting work happens. Here's the full YAML:

description: >
  Access salary reports and compensation data. Retrieve team salary
  summaries and compensation benchmarks for your department.
visibility_condition:
  objects:
    - type: ias_attributes
      attributes:
        - attribute: groups
          match: ANY
          values:
            - HR_MANAGERS
target:
  name: restricted_info
  type: function

Let's walk through every field in the visibility_condition block.

visibility_condition.objects is an array. This design allows you to specify multiple condition objects in a single scenario. When you provide more than one object, all of them must evaluate to true for the scenario to be visible. It's an implicit AND across objects. This is how you build compound conditions like "user must be in the HR_Managers group AND have a specific Work Zone navigation target available."

type: ias_attributes tells Joule to evaluate this condition against the attributes in the user's IAS identity token. IAS embeds user attributes (including group memberships) into the JWT it issues at authentication time.

attribute: groups specifies which IAS attribute to check. The groups attribute contains the list of IAS groups the authenticated user belongs to. This is the most common attribute to use for role-based access control.

match: ANY controls how the user's attribute value is compared against the values list. With ANY, the condition evaluates to true if the user belongs to at least one of the listed groups. The alternative is match: ALL, which requires the user to be a member of every group in the values list. Use ALL when you need compound group requirements, for example "must be in both HR_MANAGERS and Senior_Staff."

values is the list of IAS group names to check against. In this case, it's a single group: HR_MANAGERS. You can add multiple group names here and combine them with match: ANY to grant access to users from any of several groups. This is useful when different teams or roles should all have access to the same sensitive scenario.

Supported attributes for ias_attributes:

  • groups: IAS group membership (the most common use case)
  • email: IAS email
  • user_uuid: the unique identifier of the specific user, useful for individual-level access. This GUID can be found in IAS when viewing the user.
  • Custom attributes: any custom attribute you've configured in IAS for your users
  • sap_licenses: SAP product license assignments (available since schema version 3.23.0); enables license-based feature gating

 

Step 5: The Dialog Functions

Both functions in this example use simple message actions. In a real capability, these would call APIs, run queries, or invoke more complex action chains. Here we keep them simple so the focus stays on the visibility pattern rather than the function implementation.

The public function:

action_groups:
  - actions:
      - type: message
        message:
          type: text
          content: >
            Here is the general HR information you requested. Our company
            policies are available on the HR portal. Office locations include
            our headquarters and regional offices. The annual holiday schedule
            has been published for this year.

The restricted function:

action_groups:
  - actions:
      - type: message
        message:
          type: text
          content: >
            Here are the salary reports for your team. The compensation
            summary shows current salary bands and recent adjustments.
            This information is confidential and restricted to HR Managers.

Both functions follow the same structure: a single action_groups entry containing a single actions list with one message action of type text. The content field contains the response Joule will deliver when the function is invoked.

In production, you'd replace these static messages with api-request actions that call your actual HR APIs. The visibility_condition on the scenario works identically regardless of how complex the downstream function becomes. The access gate is enforced at the scenario layer, before the function is ever invoked.

 

Step 6: Deploy and Test

Your directory structure should look like this before deploying:

├── da.sapdas.yaml
└── capabilities/role_based_access/
    ├── capability.sapdas.yaml
    ├── scenarios/
    │   ├── public_scenario.yaml
    │   └── restricted_scenario.yaml
    └── functions/
        ├── public_info.yaml
        └── restricted_info.yaml

First, run the linter to catch any structural issues:

joule lint

Fix any errors before proceeding.

Deploy the capability:

joule deploy -c -n "hr_reports_test"

The -c flag compiles, and -n provides the assistant name. Once the deployment completes, launch it in Joule:

joule launch "hr_reports_test"

Test 1: HR Manager user: Log in to Joule as your HR Manager test user (the one assigned to the HR_Managers group in IAS). Ask something like: "Can you show me salary reports for my team?"

Joule should match the restricted scenario and return the salary report message. Both scenarios are in the candidate pool for this user, and the intent of the question clearly maps to the restricted scenario's description.

Test 2: Regular employee: Log out and log back in as your regular employee test user (not in HR_MANAGERS). Ask the same question: "Can you show me salary reports for my team?"

Joule should respond that it can't help with that request, or may return a generic fallback. The restricted scenario is not in the candidate pool at all for this user. Even if the intent perfectly matches the restricted scenario's description, Joule cannot select it.

Try asking about office locations and holiday schedules with both users. The public scenario should work identically for both.

 

Advanced Patterns

The example above covers the most common case (single group, ANY match) but visibility conditions support more sophisticated patterns.

Multiple groups with ANY match: Add additional group names to the values array to grant access to users from any of those groups. For example, granting access to both HR_Managers and Finance_Directors requires only adding Finance_Directors to the values list.

ALL match for compound group requirements: Change match: ANY to match: ALL when a user must belong to every group in the list.

Individual user access with user_uuid: Replace attribute: groups with attribute: user_uuid and provide specific user UUIDs in the values list. This pins scenario access to named individuals, which is useful for capability owners or designated testers.

Email-based access with email: Use attribute: email with specific email addresses in the values list as an alternative to user_uuid when you want to identify individuals by a more readable identifier. This is handy for small allowlists where maintaining a list of UUIDs would be impractical.

Combining multiple attributes within ias_attributes: You can list multiple attributes entries inside a single ias_attributes object. When you do this, all of them must evaluate to true for the scenario to be visible. This is an AND condition, not an OR. In the example below, a user must both belong to the HR_MANAGERS group and have the specific email address. Meeting only one of the two conditions is not enough:

visibility_condition:
  objects:
    - type: ias_attributes
      attributes:
        - attribute: groups
          match: ANY
          values:
            - HR_MANAGERS
        - attribute: email
          match: ANY
          values:
            - hrmanager@jouledev.com

License-based gating with sap_licenses (schema version 3.23.0+): Use attribute: sap_licenses to gate scenarios based on which SAP product licenses a user holds.

Combining ias_attributes with ibn_targets (schema version 3.15.0+): Add a second object to the visibility_condition.objects array with type: ibn_targets to require both group membership and Work Zone navigation target availability. Since all objects in the array must evaluate to true, this creates an AND condition across the two types.

 

Conclusion

The visibility_condition field gives you a declarative, zero-code mechanism for controlling which users can access which scenarios in your Joule capability. The access control lives entirely in the scenario YAML. There's no conditional logic in your function code, no middleware to configure, and the only changes required are to your IAS configuration (for example, creating groups).

The key points to take away:

  • Scenarios with no visibility_condition are always visible to all end users
  • ias_attributes reads directly from the IAS identity token at runtime
  • match: ANY grants access if the user is in at least one listed group; match: ALL requires membership in every listed group

In the next post in this series, we'll look at document grounding: connecting Joule to enterprise document repositories so responses are grounded in your organization's actual content.

entry.title: Is this a sign SAP is losing the AI race?
entry.content:
Most of us are aware of SAP new API policy and its effects on external AI usage. Most of the policy is certainly about legitimate concerns on API usage, and it's effects on system stability. While I'm a strong believer in the customer's ability to manage his system as he wants, overuse of APIs with millions of request can be problematic and more and more it's SAP's system not the customer's.

But then we have this: "Any AI platform can connect to SAP through endorsed architectures and pathways" and then "Any AI platform can connect to SAP through endorsed architectures and pathways, such as A2A protocol via Joule and Agent Gateway", with a reference to the SAP Architecture Center which is all about BTP, Joule and A2A (so Joule).

In summary and in my view:

  • API policy on usage: Legitimate concern regarding stability, despite the fact i ultimately believe customer should be able to do what they want with their system.
  • Mandatory A2A with Joule: If there is overusage of APIs that is already covered by the general API policy, making this additional clause seem vendor lock-in and protectionism. I've proposed A2A architectures to customers, but it shouldn't be mandatory as a matter of principle.

When do companies resort to lock-in and protectionism? When they can't compete on the open market. We have tariffs on automobiles where Europe tariffs China because BYD is too cheap, and US tariffs the EU because EU doesn't want its cars.

So why are many people resorting to external agents instead of building on Joule? Three things SAP needs to solve, and fast:

  • SAP Development with AI: Claude Code does all my javascript and python code at scale and with quality, at this point i don't code, i do specs. Code outside of SAP ecosystem is orders of magnitude cheaper because AI is bad at doing SAP code. SAP MCP servers are just a patch, not a solution.
  • Joule Skills: Joule needs to do more. I need to have a reason to use Joule besides the fact it has SAP branding, and if Joule was able to do things like "create a payment specification for item A and B" as part of the LLM knowledge (or SAP built skills), it would have a real competitive advantage. But it doesn't, and it knows as much about SAP as the general LLM that it is using. 
  • Local AI: As tokens get more expensive customers will move to self hosted AI models that handle day to day tasks. We don't need Opus or GPT-5.5 for basic interpretation. SAP does have self hosting and large enterprises will want to go down that path, but mid-range customers just want to run their AI operations in house with the hardware they have.

From my perspective i'll continue to advocate for responsible API usage and scenarios that avoid massive data scraping, which is something your ERP shouldn't be subjected to. That's the real concern here and what we should be focusing, while SAP should be focusing on making Joule better instead of trying to make it mandatory. If stability is the concern it shouldn't matter if the usage is being generated by AI or a custom Node.js program.

At the end of the day S/4 HANA is an amazing product and customers need to be able to interact with it the way they want. Make Joule smarter about SAP and customers will choose to use it above other solutions.

entry.title: Re: Multi Year Budget Consumption on PO Based Invoices No...
entry.content:
Please note VT 54 consumes budget on posting date only which is a standard behaviour. No way to force delivery-date AVC for VT 54 in Public Cloud. A workaround is to split GR/SES/MIRO postings by fiscal year, or adjust business rules to rely on commitment budget for delivery-date control.

If this requirement is essential for a long term, log an Improvement Request with SAP Influence program.

entry.title: Re: Request for Flexible Posting Period Control (Plant-wi...
entry.content:
You can consider the below approaches

1) Operational Workarounds
Post the 3rd shift production documents with the correct posting date (backdated to the last day of the month) even if the entry is made the next day. This ensures the postings hit the intended period, while the new period remains open for invoicing.

2) Dual Period Open Strategy
Keep both the closing period (e.g., April) and the new period (e.g., May) open for a short overlap window. Control usage through business process discipline rather than system restriction.

3) Custom Fields / Workflow
Use workflow or validation rules to enforce that certain document types (e.g., production postings) must carry the intended posting date. While you cannot restrict by plant, you can guide users with validations.

entry.title: Unifying ATC Check Variants in SAP Cloud ALM for Feature Export and Clean Core
entry.content:
I am seeking clarification regarding the configuration of ABAP Test Cockpit (ATC) check variants within SAP Cloud ALM, specifically in our RISE with SAP environment. Our goal is to establish a single, consistent standard for code quality that applies to both pre-deployment checks and our overall Clean Core monitoring.

We have identified two key processes where ATC checks are performed:

  • Feature Export Check: When using the Feature Export functionality in SAP Cloud ALM, an ATC check is triggered. Our understanding is that this check uses the global Code Inspector check variant from the managed system, which defaults to DEFAULT. Let's refer to this as Variant A.
  • Clean Core Dashboard: For the Clean Core Dashboard (viewable via the RISE with SAP Methodology), we import ATC results. The required checks for this dashboard focus on Clean Core principles, specifically: (1) Allowed SAP enhancement technologies, (2) Usage of APIs, (3) Critical statements. Let's refer to a custom variant containing these specific checks as Variant B.

Our assumption is that we can and should align these two processes by using the same check variant for both. This would involve creating our definitive "Clean Core" variant (Variant B) and then configuring it as the global check variant in the managed system, thereby making Variant A and Variant B identical.

I wonder if this understanding is correct? Specifically, can we configure the Feature Export ATC check to use the same custom "Clean Core" variant that we use for the Clean Core Dashboard, ensuring a unified governance approach?

entry.title: Re: Latest People Profile Enablement: What You Need to Know
entry.content:
Dear ShaveethaK,

About the text:

 "Refer to the permissions defined under Employee View Sections (only for Legacy People Profile) and provide similar permissions in People Profile Categories for each role."

 

In the blog 'Latest People Profile':

(https://community.sap.com/t5/product-and-customer-updates/latest-people-profile-experience/ba-p/13648757/page/17#comments)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

DianaLiden
Product and Topic Expert
yesterday
Hi 

Thanks for the questions. Let me respond:

Q1: Yes, please prepare your production instances. If you don’t prepare them and you wait for the 1H 2026 universal push, then all categories will be visible to all users.

Migration: the system automatically migrates your category-level access configurations and generates permission items for those categories with target roles assigned, including their role assignments in the People Profile Categories permission list. Permissions are not generated for categories that were visible to everyone.

Q2: No need to change it. We will migrate your settings.

Please refer to EC 1H 2026 release Q&A webinar (and PDF slides 43-52, 62-68):

Kind regards, Diana

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 

So if in 'Configure Latest People Profile' the category permissions are set before the production release the category-level access configurations are migrated to RBP / roles. (answer Q1).

 

Kind regards, Edwin

My questions are:

entry.title: Poland KSeF eDocument Cockpit - "The data object could not be created: The type does not exist"
entry.content:
We are implementing Poland KSeF e-invoicing on S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition using SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, following the official SAP documentation  and note 3708755. Authentication is via KSeF certificate.

The setup works perfectly in our test system. In production, however, every attempt to submit or display the XML of a Poland KSeF eInvoice in the eDocument Cockpit fails with:

"The data object could not be created: The type  does not exist. Message no. EDOCUMENT000"

Notice the double space between the words "type" and "does".

We have deleted and recreated all settings multiple times, always with identical results. We are confident the configuration is correct — the test system proves it.

Our experience with SAP Support so far has been frustrating. We first raised this case on April 9th. After nearly a month, we still have no resolution, no committed timeline, and no feedback from any technical team, with the case being bounced from one department to another. When we pushed for an update, we were informally told that the root cause is likely already known internally — yet no fix has been delivered, and no transparent communication has been proactively shared with us. For a production blocker with direct legal and financial consequences — KSeF compliance is now mandatory in Poland — this is simply not acceptable.

We would like to know:

  • Is anyone else experiencing this exact error in production while the test system works fine?
  • Has anyone found a workaround or received a fix from SAP?
  • Are there any clues about which specific component or setting causes the test/production discrepancy?

Any input is deeply appreciated

entry.title: SAP RAP: Integration of custom entity in non custom entity based CDS composition tree as facet
entry.content:
Hello,

I have a question with regards to a RAP based FIORI object page.

I have a problem to use/integrate a CDS custom entity in a "normal" RAP Object page. With "normal" I mean a RAP application, based on a CDS composition tree WITHOUT custom entities (classic parent-child composition).

My RAP artifacts:

- CDS Interface view header data
- CDS Interface view child data (connected via composition relation)
- Other CDS for Search helps
- Composition view for Header data (Root)
- Composition view for child data (dependend)
- Meta-Data Extension to composition root-view 
- Meta-Data Extension to child view
- Behavior definition for interface CDS
- Behavior Definition for consumption/projection
- Service-Definition
- Service-Binding OdataV4
- Custom Entity with Parameter + ABAP class is formerly defined

So my application IS NOT A COMPOSITION TREE OF CUSTOM ENTITIES !

RAP application is starting. Single list record could be selected, go into Detail. Detail shows header data + separate facet for child related data in UI LINEITEM_REFERENCE.

Somehow I'm failing integrating the custom entity into the "classic" composition tree. Target is a new facet, showing result
data of custom entity as a listing in a facet (like child data). Maybe there is a lack of understanding on my side.

A problem beside is, that my custom entity needs a parameter to select data for a specific (the just in RAP UI selected)
object.

Does someone has this achived and maybe some example code for
- Integration of custom entity into non-custom entity CDS tree, both for interface and consumption
- Delivering the parameter to custom entity using actual RAP detail data?

Thanks in advance.
Klaus

Payment cannot be created... because the Account Payment option is already set on the cart.

Account Payment → NO /payments
OPF Gateway → /payments

---

Any confirmation or best practice would be appreciated 🙏

Stefania

entry.title: Webi Formula for Aggregating amounts of Hierarchy
entry.content:
Hi,

We have a Webi report created sourcing from a Derived Table having a join on a standard table via Information Design Tool. Amounts not showing for "ENT" nodes due to no match available for the child nodes between two tables joined. Can you please provide a Webi formula where we can aggregate amount on the Upper Level Hierarchy ENT nodes and also those Lower Level Hierarchy Nodes.

E.g. Parent Node ENTFINAL - Child Node ENTS1234

        Parent Node ENTS1234 - Child Node ENTS2345

         Parent Node ENTS2345 - Child Node ABCD, Child Node BCDE, Child Node CDEF

Requirement is to Sum up the amounts for Child Node ABCD, Child Node BCDE, Child Node CDEF and assign to Child Node ENTS2345 part of row Parent Node ENTS1234 - Child Node ENTS2345. Then same amount will be posted on Child Node ENTS1234 part of row Parent Node ENTFINAL - Child Node ENTS1234.

entry.title: Transforming SAP APM Documentation for a Global Audience
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Asset Performance Management (APM) documentation is enhanced to make it more accessible and multilingual. Building on earlier enhancements like SAP Companion and video tutorials, the MT On-the-Fly for Delta Content (MT OTF Delta) marks a leap forward — ensuring accuracy, consistency, and up to date content across all delivery channels.

What This Means for You

  • Always current: Documentation updates happen at the same pace as product changes. Documentation pages available in 39+ languages.
  • Multilingual access: Read content in your preferred language without waiting for translations.
  • Ease of use: Switch between languages anytime, with English always available as the official reference.

Key Enhancements

  • March 2603 release
    • SAP Companion In‑App Help: Context‑sensitive panels, orientation snippets, and direct links to the SAP Help Portal.
    • Video Tutorials: Step‑by‑step guidance to help users learn processes while working.
  • Machine Translation Evolution
  • February 2025: Machine Translation on the Fly (MT on the Fly) introduced dynamic multilingual documentation, allowing users to access content in their preferred language.
  • April 2604: Building on this foundation, MT On-the-Fly for Delta Content (MT OTF Delta) delivered a leap forward. MTOTF enhances accuracy and consistency across all delivery channels, expanding usability worldwide. With this, users will gain faster access to reliable documentation in their preferred language.

Disclaimer: Keep in mind that this is an AI automatic translation tool based on the English Language. Be cautious with the translated content as it may not be perfect. When in doubt, consult the original English text.  

 How to Access Multilingual Documentation

  1. Open the SAP Help Portal → SAP Asset Performance Management | SAP Help Portal
  2. Select Your Language
    • Use the language dropdown at the top of the page (next to “Version: Cloud | English”).
    • Click the arrow and choose from available languages drop-down menu.
    • Select the desired language such as Arabic, Chinese, French, German, Spanish, and more.
  3. Switch Back to English Anytime

Conclusion

The Machine Translation On-the-Fly allows instant translation in 40 languages for SAP APM Help Portal content, enhancing your experience as you can now choose your preferred language. 

With MTOTF, SAP APM documentation is becoming more connected, consistent, and globally accessible.

📌See Also

  • SAP Help Portal Content with Machine Translation On-the-Fly
  • Read the Right Docs: sIoT vs. eIoT in SAP Asset Performance Management

entry.title: Automatic Reprocessing for Replication Errors of Business Partner Relationships in S/4HANA Cloud
entry.content:
If you are working with the Business Partner master data objects in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, you might have seen the "Relationship Failed" error while replicating the objects from one system to another. Usually, this isn't a data error, but a timing issue — the system tries to link two business partner objects before they are both fully saved in the database. 

Instead of manually restarting these messages every time an error occurs, you can use the Automatic Reprocessing app to let the system fix the issues. Here is how to set it up.

When replicating Business Partners, the Relationship Scenario fails (often with a "Locked" or "Not Found" error). This happens because the relationship record reaches the system faster than the BUSINESS PARTNER master record itself. Manually monitoring and clicking "Restart" for every failure is a waste of resources. 

 

The Solution: Configuring "Automatic Reprocessing" of Messages 

Follow these three steps to automate the recovery process using the Fiori-native framework. 

 

Step 1: Open the Automatic Reprocessing App 

  1. Log in to your SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition system with the role SAP_BR_CONF_EXPERT_BUS_NET_INT or similar integration roles to access the app. 

  2. Use the App Finder (or the search bar) to locate the app "Automatic Reprocessing". 


Step 2: Maintain Reprocessing Entries 

This is where you define the "Retry Rules." 

  1. Click on Create Entry. 

  2. Identify your Interface: Maintain the Namespace and Interface name related to your BUSINESS PARTNER Relationship (e.g., for SOAP APIs). 

  3. Define the Error: Enter the specific Message Class and Message Number that is causing the failure (e.g., Message Class R1 or MDG_BS_BUSINESS PARTNER_MS).
            
  4. Set the Frequency: 
  • Max Retries: How many times should the system try again? (Recommended: 3-5). 
  • Waiting Time: How long should the system wait between tries? (Recommended: 60 - 300 seconds). 

Pro Tip: These are autosaved entries. Once you fill in the fields, the system registers them immediately. There is no manual "Save" button to click—your changes are live as soon as they are entered. 

 

Step 3: Retrigger and Monitor 

After you maintain the configuration:

  1. Retrigger the Scenario: Go back to the source system or the Message Monitoring app and retrigger the failed relationship record. 

  2. Watch the Status: The message will move from "Error" to "In Process" or "Scheduled for Reprocessing." 

  3. The system will now automatically attempt to create the relationship based on your wait times, giving the Business Partner objects time to be created first. 

Conclusion 

By spending a few minutes in the Automatic Reprocessing app, you eliminate hours of manual monitoring. This approach ensures your BUSINESS PARTNER relationships stay consistent without human intervention.

entry.title: Authentication and Identity Management in SAP Signavio: Where We Are and What's Next
entry.content:
If you manage users across a growing software landscape, you know the feeling. Another spreadsheet upload. Another manual onboarding step. Another moment of uncertainty during an audit when someone asks, "Who had access to what, and when?" Identity management is one of those invisible foundations — it rarely makes the highlight reel, but when it works well, everything else gets easier. 

We've been listening. And we're doing something about it.  

Last year, we announced our plans to harmonize authentication and identity management across the SAP Signavio Process Transformation Suite. Today, we want to share what's happened since: where we are now, what we're solving, and what's coming next.
 
The Challenges That Got Us Here

As SAP Signavio expanded into a multi-application suite, identity management needed to keep pace. Administrators managing large user populations told us they wanted less manual work — fewer CSV uploads, more automation, and a single interface for onboarding and offboarding across applications. 

Security expectations evolved too. Customers increasingly needed native Multi-Factor Authentication, richer audit trails, and easier ways to demonstrate compliance during reviews. These are table-stakes capabilities in today's landscape, and we wanted to deliver them as part of the core platform. 

On the experience side, authentication and user management had grown organically across SAP Signavio applications. Bringing consistency to that — one identity, one login, one source of truth — was a clear opportunity to reduce friction for both admins and end-users. 

But the most compelling reason to invest here was what it enables. The cross-suite innovations our customers are asking for all depend on a unified identity foundation. Getting this right isn't just operational hygiene; it's what makes the next wave of capabilities possible. 

This direction was shaped directly by feedback from customers, support teams, and internal stakeholders. It demanded a fundamentally different approach — and that's exactly what we've built.

 

What We're Delivering  and Why It Matters

The answer is SAP Cloud Identity Services (SCI) — SAP's strategic platform for cloud-based identity management, built on open industry standards and already trusted across the broader SAP ecosystem. What does this mean to you? Enterprise-grade capabilities: Single Sign-On, Multi-Factor Authentication, directory integration via SCIM, and audit logging, all included. 

 

Where Are We Now

We've moved well beyond plans and prototypes. Here's what's already live and working: 

For new workspaces, SAP Cloud Identity Services integration is active and operational. That means out-of-the-box Single Sign-On (SSO), the ability to enforce Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA), and automatic syncing of user attributes and group assignments at login. These capabilities are in production today. 

But it's not just the features — it's the experience of shift they represent. Customers creating new workspaces are starting from a fundamentally stronger baseline: security by default, streamlined administration, and an identity layer designed to support everything that comes next. The groundwork is laid down. Now we're building on it. 

 

What's Coming Next
The work isn't done — and we're transparent about that. Here's what we're focused on:
  • A smooth path for existing customers: Transitions require care. We're building a guided migration path to bring existing workspaces onto SCI, with close collaboration from your Customer Success Partners throughout the process.
  • Centralized user lifecycle management: One place to provision, manage, and deprovision users across all SAP Signavio applications — a single, streamlined interface for the full user lifecycle.
  • Enterprise-scale directory integration:  Full SCIM v2 support for bulk user and group operations, enabling seamless synchronization with corporate directories like Microsoft Entra ID.
  • Audit and compliance capabilities:Comprehensive logging for login events, access changes, and user lifecycle operations — giving your organization the transparency it needs during audits and beyond.
  • And the bigger picture: This identity foundation is a steppingstone. It unlocks future initiatives like harmonized Role & Access Management, unified API authentication, and seamless cross-SAP experiences. The feature requests you've been sharing with us? This is what makes them possible. 

 

What This Means for You
  • If you're an IT administrator,  this means less manual work, a stronger security posture, and one interface instead of many. It means having the tools to prove compliance during audits without scrambling for data.
  • If you're an end user, this means one login and a consistent experience across every SAP Signavio application. No more juggling credentials or navigating different authentication flows.
  • If you're a decision-maker, this means enterprise-grade security and compliance built into your SAP Signavio investment — reduced operational overhead today, and a future-proof identity foundation that grows with your SAP landscape.

 

This Is a Journey — and Your Voice Shapes It

We're rolling this out in phases because getting it right matters more than getting it fast. Every step has been informed by the feedback you've shared — through support conversations, customer advisory boards, and direct engagement with our teams. That feedback will continue to shape what comes next. As new capabilities go live, we'll keep you informed. If you're interested in understanding how and when your SAP Signavio workspace transitions, we recommend starting the conversation now with your Customer Success Partner. Early planning makes adoption smoother — and helps us prioritize your needs as we roll out the next phases.

We're building this for you. And we're just getting started.

entry.title: Understanding Tax Code Determination in Purchase Orders (SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud)
entry.content:
While working with Purchase Orders in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, we recently faced an issue that initially caused some confusion: Purchase Order items without a tax code.

Our original assumption was that tax codes would always be populated at item level. However, we observed several PO items where the tax code was completely empty. This blog post outlines our analysis, explains the underlying tax code determination logic, and shares practical guidelines to help avoid such situations.

Standard Tax Code Determination Logic

During our investigation, we found the relevant explanation in SAP Note 501054 – FAQ: Taxes in Purchasing. The note clarifies how tax codes are determined in purchase orders.

“1. Question: How is the tax code determined in purchase orders?

Answer:

  • In the standard system, the system considers the following sources for the tax code (in this sequence): Reference item, contract, request for quotation, info record. It can also be entered manually.
  • Furthermore, the tax code can be automatically determined via the condition technique. This then has priority. (…) With Transactions MEK1/MEK2, depending on tax indicators (material, plant, account assignment, origin) you can define different tax codes in the condition tables (…)”

Based on the information from the SAP Note, the logic can be summarised as follows:

 

Determination Sequence

 

Important: The tax code determination happens at the time the purchase order item is created. Changes to master data afterwards (for example, updating a purchasing info record) do not automatically update existing PO items. To subsequently update the purchase order item, it is necessary to do so via App "Change Purchase Order - Advanced" > Item > Conditions > Update (see screenshot below).

 

PO Tax Code Guidelines

After understanding the logic, we defined the following guidelines to prevent empty tax codes in purchase orders.

1. Maintain All Relevant Tax Condition Records (TTX1)

Responsible: Accounting

Ensure that all required tax condition records are maintained for the relevant key combinations (e.g. material, plant, account assignment, country of origin) in the app Set Tax Rates – Purchasing (MEK2).

2. Maintain Tax Codes in Reference Documents

Responsible: Purchase Requisitioners / Procurement

Tax codes should be explicitly maintained in:

  • Purchase Requisitions
  • Purchase Contracts
  • Requests for Quotation

This ensures that the tax code is passed correctly to the purchase order.

3. Maintain Tax Codes in Purchasing Info Records

Responsible: Procurement

Purchasing info records should contain valid tax codes to act as a fallback if the tax code can't be defined based on the reference document .

4. Prevent Saving Without a Tax Code Using a BAdI

Responsible: SAP Application Management

If maintaining a tax code is mandatory, a BAdI can be implemented to prevent saving a PO item without a tax code.

Example using BAdI MM_PUR_S4_PO_MODIFY_ITEM:

* Sample Code

    DATA: ls_message LIKE LINE OF messages.
...
  IF purchaseorderitem-taxcode IS INITIAL.
    ls_message-messagetype = 'E'.
    ls_message-messagevariable1 = 'Please maintain a tax code for the item'.
    ls_message-messageid = 'MEPO' .
    ls_message-messagenumber = 250.
    APPEND ls_message TO messages.
  ENDIF.

* END OF Sample Code
 
This ensures data consistency even if master data maintenance is incomplete.

Summary

Tax code determination in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud follows a clear but often underestimated logic. If tax condition records, reference documents, and purchasing info records are not consistently maintained, purchase order items may end up without a tax code.

By:

  • Understanding the determination sequence,
  • Maintaining master and reference data carefully,
  • And (if necessary) enforcing mandatory fields via BAdIs,

you can effectively prevent empty tax codes and ensure compliant, consistent purchasing processes.

References

  • 501054 - FAQ: Taxes in purchasing - SAP for Me
  • Set Tax Rates - Purchasing | SAP Help Portal
  • 3743094 - Automatic tax code determination in purchase order (PO) creation does not function as expected - SAP for Me

entry.title: LMS - Issues with course completion
entry.content:
Hi Community,

We recently launched a global course for approximately 10,000 users, and we are experiencing multiple issues with content completion. Even when users reach the end of the course, completion is not recorded, and they are redirected back to the beginning of the course.

The course was created in Articulate. The SCORM packages are configured to mark completion once the user completes 90% of the content and are exported in SCORM 1.2 format. Each SCORM package is relatively large (200–400 MB) and contains multiple videos created in Synthesia.

I am wondering whether this issue could be related to LMS configuration settings or whether the large file size and content complexity are negatively impacting performance.

 

Thank you, 

Anna

entry.title: Re: SoCal Community Event : SAP Business Data Cloud " From Pipelines to Data Products "
entry.content: Hi, not sure if this is the right place to post this question. At my company we have been using DataSphere (DSP) for quite some time. Our S4 is not yet in RISE. In DSP we have created good Analytic models with specific business logic, pre-defined measures in the model, rich semantics, mixing data from multiple sources (SAP and non-SAP), etc. Our tables are currently stored in standard memory / disk, not data lake. We would like to transition into data products and also being able to use BDC Connect for external consumption. My question / topic is how to do this transition. Based on the documentation I´ve read BDC connect can only connect to Data Products based on Data Lake file storage so I guess I will have to transition (not an obvious change). Additionally I´ve seen demos and documents where I can see you can create Data Products from tables (and I´ve also seen from views but haven't tested) but not from an existing Analytic Model. So basically I would like to discuss options to transition our existing DSP models into Data Products that can be used directly or consumed via BSC Connect. Is the upcoming Data Product Studio going to help with this?

entry.title: How to Establish a Multi-Impact Environmental Footprint with SAP
entry.content:
Why Multi-Impact Footprint Matters

Focusing only on carbon emissions provides an incomplete picture of environmental impact. Organizations increasingly need to consider additional factors such as water usage, resource depletion, and other environmental indicators.

SAP Sustainability Footprint Management enables companies to go beyond carbon and calculate multi-impact environmental footprints, offering a more holistic view of sustainability performance.

With multi-impact insights, businesses can:

  • Assess environmental impact across multiple categories
  • Identify hidden hotspots beyond carbon emissions
  • Make more informed sustainability decisions
  • Strengthen overall environmental performance

What You’ll Learn in This Course

This focused, intermediate-level course introduces the key concepts and calculations for multi-impact footprints:

  1. Understanding Multi-Impact Categories
    Learn the fundamentals of different environmental impact categories and how they complement carbon footprint analysis.
  2. Calculating Multi-Impact Footprints
    Discover how to calculate and analyze environmental impacts across multiple categories using SAP Sustainability Footprint Management.

You’ll also learn how to identify hotspots and derive insights to support targeted improvement initiatives.

Who Should Take the Course?

This learning journey is ideal for:

  • Business Users
  • Consultants

Prerequisites:

  • Establishing Corporate Carbon Footprint Emissions with Simple Configuration
  • Establishing Corporate Carbon Footprint Emissions with Advanced Configuration
  • Establishing the Product Carbon Footprint with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management

Go beyond carbon and gain a complete environmental perspective and explore the Course “Establishing the Multi-impact Environmental Footprint with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management” here.

entry.title: SAP BTP - CI - Immutable Routing Principle: in SAP CPI with Value Mapping metadata ODCP in practice
entry.content:
"The artifact is immutable. The metadata is the architecture."

Introduction

If you have ever worked with SAP PI/PO, you know how elegant the IDoc landscape can be. A single tRFC destination. A single IDoc port. All IDocs from all systems flowing through one gateway, with routing handled centrally in the Integration Directory. Adding a new IDoc type meant adding a configuration entry — not creating a new interface.

When teams migrate to SAP BTP Integration Suite (CPI), that elegance often disappears. The common approach creates one iFlow per IDoc type, one endpoint per interface, one SAP port per destination. A landscape with 20 IDoc types across 3 SAP systems quickly becomes 60 iFlows, 60 ports, and 60 deployment cycles. Any new IDoc type or new sender system requires a developer, a change request, and a redeployment. This is what ODCP calls Package Sprawl and Endpoint Chaos.

This post presents a pattern that restores that elegance in CPI — and goes further. It is based on the same architectural principle applied in SAP BTP API Management through the GDCR (Gateway Domain-Centric Routing) and DDCR (Domain Driven-Centric Router) patterns: the routing artifact is immutable. What grows is the metadata.

In API Management, the proxy never changes — the KVM evolves.
In SAP CPI, the iFlow never changes — the Value Mapping evolves.

Same principle. Two layers of the same architecture.

⚠ everything here should be adopt, this is a basic presentation.

The video:

 

The main flow to receive all the IDOCS from any company e division: id01.shared.routing.sap.idoc.pd.f.in.async

The second iflow inside each Domain package, in this case Sales O2C: id00.o2c.routing.pd.idoc.iflow.f.in.async

The logic is the same just change the parameters in the groovy script regards the Value Mapping table, there are two, one core, and one inside each package this granulation

 

⚠ Adaptation Notice

This pattern is designed within the SDIA governance framework using ODCP naming. If your organisation does not follow SDIA/ODCP, adapt the package names, iFlow names, and VM artifact names to your convention. The routing logic and Groovy script work independently of naming.

To adopt ODCP fully: DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18876593

The Same Principle — Two Layers

The GDCR pattern in API Management uses the DDCR engine to resolve routing at runtime from a KVM. The proxy never changes. Key format: sales.order.create.sfdc → https://target/.... p50 = 0.34ms — sub-millisecond, zero regex, zero hardcode.

The same principle in CPI: one immutable iFlow, one Value Mapping that grows. Key format: ORDERS05.S4HCLNT100.100 → /pd/sales/o2c/order-inbound.

 

The Architecture — Two Immutable Layers

The solution uses two routing layers. Both follow the same pattern. Only the Value Mapping content differs.

SAP systems (any IDoc)
    ↓  [single SOAP endpoint]
Layer 1 — id01.shared.sap.idoc.pd.f.in.async    ← IMMUTABLE
    ↓  vm.shared.idoc.routing
ProcessDirect → Sales package · Finance package · Logistics package
    ↓
Layer 2 — id01.sales.pd.idoc.pd.f.in.async      ← IMMUTABLE
    ↓  vm.sales.o2c.idoc.routing
ProcessDirect → specific processing iFlow

The same Groovy script (sc.shared.idoc.channel-builder) runs at both layers. Zero code changes when a new IDoc type or new sender system is added.

The main IDOC VM - vm.shared.idoc.routing

The VM local in the Sales O2C - CPI Package - id00.o2c.vm.routing.idoc.metadata

The Composite Key — Three IDoc Header Fields

The routing decision is built from three fields of the IDoc Control Record (EDI_DC40), extracted by the upstream Content Modifier via native CPI XPath:

IDOC_TYP  ← //*[local-name()='IDOCTYP']/text()    e.g. ORDERS05
SNDSYS    ← //*[local-name()='SNDPRN']/text()      e.g. S4HCLNT100
MANDT ← //*[local-name()='MANDT']/text()      e.g. 100

The Groovy assembles the composite key:

def vmKey = "${idocTypRaw}.${sndSysRaw}".${sndSysRaw}"
// "ORDERS05.S4HCLNT100.100" → /pd/sales/o2c/order-inbound

One key. One lookup. One result. The SNDPRN identifies the sender division — S4HCLNT100 is acme, ERP1402 is acme eu — so the same IDoc type routes differently per division. No extra fields needed.

Value Mapping Configuration

Artifact: vm.shared.idoc.routing

Agency (source) Identifier (source) Source value → Target value

sap.idocidoc.control.typeORDERS05.S4HCLNT100.100→/pd/sales/o2c/order-inbound
sap.idocidoc.control.typeINVOIC02.S4HCLNT100.001→/pd/finance/r2r/invoice-inbound
sap.idocidoc.control.typeORDERS05.ERP1402.874→/pd/sales/o2c/order-inbound
sap.idocidoc.control.typeORDERS05.*→/pd/shared/idoc/deadletter

Agency semantics: sap.idoc — SAP owns the IDoc type code list. enterprise.integration — your company owns the routing target.

⚠ Source values are case-sensitive and must match the IDoc Control Record exactly — uppercase for IDOCTYP (ORDERS05, not orders05).

The composition of the key should also be adapt in your company, this is just sample.

The Groovy Script

// =============================================================================
//  SCRIPT  : IDoc Generic Channel Builder via VM metadata
//  AUTHOR  : Ricardo Viana — SAP BTP Integration Suite Expert
//  VERSION : 1.0.0
//  DATE    : 2026-05-06
// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
//  DESCRIPTION
//  -----------
//  This script is part of a Generic IDoc Routing architecture on SAP BTP
//  Integration Suite (CPI). It acts as the dynamic routing decision engine
//  inside a generic inbound IDoc iFlow, enabling a single integration
//  endpoint to receive ANY IDoc type from ANY SAP source system and route
//  it to the correct Process Direct channel — without any hardcoded logic.
//
//  PRE-REQUISITES
//  --------------
//  Content Modifier (before this script) must set the following exchange
//  properties using native CPI XPath expressions:
//    IDOC_TYP   ← //*[local-name()='IDOCTYP']/text()
//    IDOC_EXTN  ← //*[local-name()='CIMTYP']/text()
//    MSG_TYP    ← //*[local-name()='MESTYP']/text()
//    SNDSYS     ← //*[local-name()='SNDPRN']/text()
//
//
//  NOTE: Source values in the Value Mapping must match the EXACT case of
//  the values in the IDoc Control Record (typically UPPERCASE for IDOCTYP
//  and the sender system name as registered in SAP).
//
//  PROCESS DIRECT CHANNEL
//  ----------------------
//  The downstream Process Direct receiver channel must be configured with
//  the dynamic address expression: ${property.TARGET_CHANNEL}
//  The base path (e.g. /generic/routing/idoc) is set directly on the
//  listener channel — not in this script.
//
//  FALLBACK BEHAVIOUR
//  ------------------
//  If the composite key is not found in the Value Mapping, TARGET_CHANNEL
//  is set to "unknown" and IS_MAPPED is set to "false". A dedicated Process
//  Direct listener at that address should handle dead-letter alerting.
// =============================================================================

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import com.sap.it.api.ITApiFactory
import com.sap.it.api.mapping.ValueMappingApi

def Message processData(Message message) {

    // =========================================================================
    //  SECTION 1 — VALUE MAPPING COORDINATES
    //  These four constants are the only configuration point in this script.
    //  All routing logic lives exclusively in the Value Mapping artifact.
    //  Update these values only if the VM artifact is renamed or restructured.
    // =========================================================================
    def VM_AGENCY_src='sap.idoc'        // Agency that owns the IDoc type codes (SAP source)
    def VM_ID_src='idoc.control.idoctype'   // Identifier: composite key IDOCTYP.SNDPRN
    def VM_AGENCY_TGT = 'sales.o2c'    // Agency that owns the routing target
    def VM_ID_TGT     = 'pd.routing.sales.o2c'  // Identifier: full Process Direct channel address
    def FALLBACK      = 'unknown'        // Dead-letter value when no VM entry is found


    // =========================================================================
    //  SECTION 2 — READ EXCHANGE PROPERTIES
    //  Retrieve values set by the upstream Content Modifier via native XPath.
    //  Using direct map access (props[key]) is faster than getProperty(key)
    //  because it avoids the overhead of the generic property resolution chain.
    //  RAW variables preserve the original case for the Value Mapping lookup.
    //  Normalised variables are lowercased for downstream process consistency.
    // =========================================================================
    def props = message.getProperties()

    // Preserve original case — required to match VM entries (e.g. "ORDERS05")
    def idocTypRaw = (props['IDOC_TYP'] as String)?.trim() ?: ''
    def mandSys  = (props['MANDT']   as String)?.trim() ?: ''
    def sndSysRaw  = (props['SNDSYS']   as String)?.trim() ?: ''

    // =========================================================================
    //  SECTION 3 — COMPOSITE KEY ASSEMBLY
    //  The lookup key is built by concatenating IDOCTYP and SNDPRN with a dot
    //  separator. This strategy enables per-division routing using a single
    //  Value Mapping group, without requiring separate VM artifacts per division.
    //
    //  Format  : {IDOCTYP}.{SNDPRN}.{MANDT}
    //  Examples: ORDERS05.ERP1402.100  |  INVOIC02.SAP849.001  |  DELVRY07.ERP1402.150
    //
    //  Both components retain their original case to match the VM entries
    //  exactly — the Value Mapping API performs a case-sensitive comparison.
    // =========================================================================
    def vmKey = "${idocTypRaw}.${sndSysRaw}.${mandSys}"


    // =========================================================================
    //  SECTION 4 — VALUE MAPPING LOOKUP
    //  Single API call resolves the composite key to the full Process Direct
    //  channel address. Using ITApiFactory.getApi() is the recommended and
    //  most performant way to access CPI APIs in Groovy scripts — the runtime
    //  caches the API instance, so repeated calls within the same worker thread
    //  do not incur object creation overhead.
    //  On lookup failure (key not found or VM not deployed), the script does
    //  NOT throw — it falls back gracefully and logs the failure detail.
    // =========================================================================
    def targetChannel = FALLBACK
    def vmError       = ''

    try {
        def vmApi  = ITApiFactory.getApi(ValueMappingApi.class, null)
        def mapped = vmApi.getMappedValue(
            VM_AGENCY_SRC, VM_ID_SRC, vmKey,   // source: composite key
            VM_AGENCY_TGT, VM_ID_TGT           // target: Process Direct address
        )

        // Assign only if the VM returned a non-blank value
        if (mapped?.trim()) targetChannel = mapped.trim()

    } catch (Exception e) {
        // Capture the error message for MPL logging — never propagate the
        // exception so the iFlow can route to the dead-letter channel instead
        // of generating a hard technical fault
        vmError = e.message ?: 'unknown error'
    }


    // =========================================================================
    //  SECTION 5 — SET OUTPUT PROPERTIES
    //  TARGET_CHANNEL  : consumed by the Process Direct receiver channel via
    //                    the dynamic address expression ${property.TARGET_CHANNEL}
    //  IS_MAPPED       : flag for downstream Router steps or monitoring alerts
    //  VM_KEY          : stored for full traceability in the MPL and alerting
    // =========================================================================
    props.put('TARGET_CHANNEL', targetChannel)
    props.put('IS_MAPPED',      (targetChannel != FALLBACK).toString())
    props.put('VM_KEY',         vmKey)


    // =========================================================================
    //  SECTION 6 — MPL OBSERVABILITY LOG
    //  Writes a structured attachment to the Message Processing Log visible
    //  in the CPI Operations Monitor. Provides full routing traceability:
    //  input values, composite key used, VM outcome, and final decision.
    //  The logger null-check avoids failures when trace level is not active.
    // =========================================================================
    messageLogFactory.getMessageLog(message)?.addAttachmentAsString(
        'IDoc Channel Builder — Routing Decision',
        """\
╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║         IDoc Generic Channel Builder  v2.0.0         ║
║         Ricardo Viana — SAP BTP Integration Expert   ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝

── INPUT (from Content Modifier) ────────────────────────
  IDOC_TYP  (raw)  : ${idocTypRaw}

── VALUE MAPPING LOOKUP ──────────────────────────────────
  Agency  (source) : ${VM_AGENCY_SRC}
  Identif.(source) : ${VM_ID_SRC}
  Composite key    : ${vmKey}
  Agency  (target) : ${VM_AGENCY_TGT}
  Identif.(target) : ${VM_ID_TGT}
  VM error         : ${vmError ?: 'none'}

── ROUTING DECISION ──────────────────────────────────────
  TARGET_CHANNEL   : ${targetChannel}
  IS_MAPPED        : ${targetChannel != FALLBACK}
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
""",
        'text/plain'
    )

    return message
}

ProcessDirect receiver address: ${property.TARGET_CHANNEL}

Exception Handling — Pluggable by Design

The pattern defines where exceptions go. What happens there is a company decision based on IDoc SLA.

When IS_MAPPED = false → TARGET_CHANNEL = 'unknown' → ProcessDirect routes to the dead-letter listener.

Option Implementation

A — MinimalData Store + manual replay via CPI Operations
B — AlertSend Email / Teams / Slack with VM_KEY and IDoc type
C — RetryJMS Queue with exponential backoff
D — FullData Store + Alert + JMS (enterprise grade)

⚠ The dead-letter listener must exist as a deployed iFlow before activating the FALLBACK. Start with Option A.

Before and After

  Traditional This pattern

Endpoint1 per IDoc type per system1 for everything
New IDoc typeNew iFlow + port + deployAdd 1 VM row
New SAP systemNew destination + portAdd VM rows
Routing logicHardcoded in iFlowValue Mapping only
TraceabilityPer-iFlow MPLVM_KEY in every MPL log

Conclusion

The iFlow never changes. The Value Mapping grows.
The proxy never changes. The KVM grows.

Same principle. Layer 1 (GDCR) and Layer 3 (ODCP) of the same architecture — SDIA.

When your landscape adds a new IDoc type, a new SAP system, or a new business division: add rows to a table. No developer. No deployment. No SAP port reconfiguration.

The iFlow is the contract. The Value Mapping is the configuration. They must never be confused.
